title: Roast gammon
subtitle: Simmered, glazed, and baked to a sticky crust
excerpt: A whole gammon joint simmered in water with aromatics to draw out the salt, then glazed with mustard and brown sugar and finished in a hot oven to form a sticky, caramelised crust. Served hot or cold. A 2 kg joint feeds eight people generously and produces excellent leftovers.
sourceNotes: Glazed gammon is a British Christmas and Easter centrepiece, often served alongside or instead of turkey. The simmer-then-glaze method is standard: gammon is cured pork, often heavily salted, and the initial simmer removes the excess salt before the glaze is applied. The scored diamond pattern on the fat is traditional and provides more surface area for the glaze to cling to.

content.text: Gammon varies in how salty it is. If unsure, soak the joint in cold water for several hours before cooking, or change the simmering water after the first 30 minutes. Taste a small piece of the meat after simmering, if it is very salty, the desalting step was insufficient.

The scoring on the fat serves two purposes: it gives the glaze more surface area to adhere to, and it allows the fat to render during the oven phase. Score in a diamond pattern, cutting through the fat but not into the meat, and stud each diamond with a clove if you like.

Place the {{gammon}} in a large pan with the {{onion}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{peppercorns-black}}. Cover with cold water. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer. Simmer for 20 minutes per 500 g (80 minutes for a 2 kg joint). Remove from the water and allow to cool enough to handle. Heat the oven to 200°C. Carefully peel away the rind, leaving the fat layer intact. Score the fat in a diamond pattern with a sharp knife. Mix the {{soft-brown-sugar}} and {{dijon-mustard}} together. Spread the glaze all over the scored fat. Place in a roasting tin and roast for 25 to 30 minutes, until the glaze is deeply caramelised and sticky. Watch it carefully, the high sugar content means it can burn quickly. Rest for 15 minutes before slicing or carving.

items.fix: Reduce to 180°C and cover with foil if burning. The glaze should darken and become sticky over 25 to 30 minutes, not char in 10
items.cause: Oven too hot or gammon too close to the element
items.symptom: Glaze burning before it caramelises properly

items.fix: Change the simmering water after the first 30 minutes and continue with fresh water. Soaking overnight in cold water before cooking removes significantly more salt
items.cause: Gammon heavily cured, or simmer water not changed
items.symptom: Meat still very salty after simmering

items.fix: The scoring must go through the full fat layer. Apply glaze generously. It should coat the surface thickly. Brush on a second layer after 15 minutes of roasting
items.cause: Fat not scored deeply enough, or not enough glaze applied
items.symptom: Fat layer soft and flabby rather than caramelised

content.text: Glazed gammon is eaten hot at Christmas alongside turkey, or served cold at Boxing Day and Easter gatherings with pickles, bread, and mustard. A 2 kg joint provides enough cold meat for sandwiches for several days. Unlike most roasts, gammon is as good cold as it is hot, possibly better, since the glaze firms up and intensifies as it cools.
